title: Inverse supersymmetry in finite temperature Bose-Fermi mixtures
authors: Zachary Gazzillo, Carlos A. R. Sá de Melo
abstract: We investigate nearly degenerate Bose-Fermi mixtures and show that the breaking of generalized supersymmetry (gSUSY) between bosons and fermions, with up to two internal states, manifests itself through the emergence of fermionic Goldstino modes with up to four flavors. In particular, we draw a distinction between typical supersymmetry (SUSY), where bosons have pseudospin 0 and fermions have pseudospin 1/2, and inverse supersymmetry (iSUSY), where bosons have pseudospin 1/2 and fermions have pseudospin 0. In such systems, we highlight that the Goldstino pseudospin is carried by either its constituent fermion (SUSY) or boson (iSUSY). We then distinguish between these two cases by depicting their differing effects on the spectral functions of the bosonic and fermionic atomic species. Lastly, we propose radio-frequency- or microwave-spectroscopy experiments, analogous to momentum (angular) resolved photoemission in condensed matter physics, to measure the pseudospin-dependent spectral functions and detect the emergence of Goldstino modes in mixtures of $^{39}$K and $^{40}$K.
summary: The paper theoretically explores how breaking generalized supersymmetry in Bose-Fermi mixtures leads to the appearance of fermionic Goldstino modes. It rigorously distinguishes between standard and inverse SUSY by analyzing their distinct signatures in measurable spectral functions. The findings propose specific radio-frequency spectroscopy experiments using alkali atom mixtures to detect these exotic quantum phenomena.
main_problem: The paper investigates the emergence of fermionic Goldstino modes due to the breaking of generalized supersymmetry (gSUSY) in Bose-Fermi mixtures.
main_result: It distinguishes between standard SUSY and inverse SUSY (iSUSY), showing that iSUSY predicts unique, experimentally distinguishable features in the spectral functions of bosonic species.
method: The analysis uses Random Phase Approximation (RPA) to calculate correlators and derive Goldstino dispersion relations, comparing theoretical predictions with spectroscopic measurements.
model_or_system: The system is a nearly degenerate Bose-Fermi mixture of ultracold quantum gases (specifically $^{39}	ext{K}$ bosons and $^{40}	ext{K}$ fermions) possessing internal spin states (pseudospin).
key_observables: Goldstino dispersion $\omega = D_{ij}(p)$, bosonic/fermionic spectral functions $A(p, \omega)$ measurable via RF or microwave spectroscopy.
important_parameters: The analysis depends on the relative strengths of interactions ($U_{bf}$) and gSUSY breaking parameters ($\Delta\mu_{ij}, \chi$).
main_assumptions: Approximations include using RPA, expanding in small symmetry-breaking parameters ($\chi$), and assuming uniform particle density.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the Bose-Fermi correlator (Fig 1), the energy difference $\Delta\omega_{ij}$ vs. momentum for SUSY/iSUSY (Fig 2), self-energy contributions (Fig 3), and spectral functions $A(p, \omega)$ showing distinct features for both SUSY and iSUSY cases (Fig 4, Fig 5).
paper_structure: The paper builds from defining gSUSY in the Hamiltonian to using RPA to find Goldstino poles. It then systematically compares the predictions of standard SUSY versus inverse SUSY by analyzing how these symmetries manifest in the momentum-dependent spectral functions.
why_interesting: This work connects abstract concepts from high-energy physics (supersymmetry) directly to observable phenomena in ultracold atomic systems, providing a concrete theoretical framework for novel spectroscopic measurements.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. The RPA Bose-Fermi correlator GRPA ij , the poles of which form the Goldstino propagator Gq ij, consisting of the mean-field Bose-Fermi correlator GMF ij , and products thereof connected by the Bose-Fermi interacting strength, U bf ij . Here, the solid (dashed) lines represent the independent fermion (boson) propagator.

FIG. 2. Plots of the energy difference ∆ωij = ω −ζij(0, 0) between the energy of excitations ω and the zero-momentum particle-hole continuum boundary ζij(0, 0) versus momentum modulus |p|. In each plot, ϵF and kF represent the Fermi en- ergy and Fermi momentum, respectively. We use parameters of Tab. I to illustrate SUSY in panels (a) and (b), as well as iSUSY in panels (c) and (d). The solid black lines represent the Goldstino dispersions ω = Dij(p), while the shaded pink area describes the particle-hole continuum with the dashed red line being its boundary. Finally, the dotted gray line is the approximate zero-momentum solution ω = Dij(0) ≈Ωij.

FIG. 3. The self-energy diagrams Σb(f) ij contributing to the energy of a boson (fermion) in spin i (j) emerging due to the existence of the (i, j) Goldstino with propagator Gq ij repre- sented by the wavy line. The solid (dashed) lines describe the mean-field corrected fermion (boson) propagator.
FIG. 4. Plots of spectral functions Ab i(p, ω) for all present species of bosons. The SUSY case is shown in (a), and the iSUSY case is illustrated in (b).
FIG. 5. Plots of spectral functions Af j (p, ω) for all present species of fermions. The SUSY case is shown in (a), and the iSUSY case is illustrated in (b).
